content:
Welcome to the final week of June 2017's ICT mentorship content. This week's lessons focus on ICT stock
trading, specifically 'Seasonals & Monthly Swings'.

- SOURCE AND SCOPE
The Dow Jones Industrial seasonal tendency is credited to Moore Research, specifically Steve Moore, who
provides the best seasonal tendencies for active traders. I look at the overall directional seasonal for the
Dow Jones Industrial Average.
While one can examine NASDAQ and S&P 500, the simplest approach for me was to focus on the Dow
Jones Industrial. It's a small sample size of 30 blue-chip companies, some of the biggest in North America.
If they perform well, generally the S&P will also do well. NASDAQ, despite its tech-heavy nature, can still
be a good barometer for the overall stock market.

section_title: DOW VS. S&P 500 IN SEASONAL ANALYSIS
content:
Personally, I believe the seasonal tendency of the Dow Jones Industrial is very closely mirrored to that of
the S&P 500. The S&P 500 is a more accurate depiction of what the stock market is doing. We will refer to
this basic generic seasonal tendency implied by the Dow but will also use it with the S&P 500 in later
lessons. This allows us to filter out strengths or weaknesses in the averages to bolster confidence in higher
or lower prices.

section_title: THREE DIVISIONS IN THE TRADING YEAR
content:
- EQUITIES
There are three divisions in the year when it comes to stock trading. Many people trade stocks or invest
more actively than they should, or think they understand stocks when they don't. This lesson alone will
position you ahead in equities trading.
- The first half of the year generally presents a large or high magnitude period. This means there's a lot of
volatility, but it's directionally driven, generally bullish.

The second portion of the year to discuss is the last quarter. This is generally and primarily a bullish time of
the year. I've explained in detail that the last portion of the calendar year in the U.S. is strong due to
numerous holidays and year-end spending. This generates significant energy, showing a strong contrast in
magnitude and velocity for upward movement compared to the first portion of the year.

The last and most critical period to understand is the middle portion, boxed in. This is referred to as the low
magnitude period, beginning in May and ending in October.
From May to October, you generally see less directionally driven markets. This doesn't mean there won't be
short-term directional biases or opportunities, but if you're trading:
- Use a lot less leverage.
- If trading options, engage in a lot less activity.
- Don't be aggressive during these periods.
This period encompasses summer months, with seasonal lulls in spending due to vacations. You'll observe
many cyclical and non-cyclical events yearly. During these months, look for a range-bound consolidation
environment overall. While individual months have their own seasonal tendencies, you primarily want to
focus on being an active trader from October to year-end, and from February to May.

section_title: DOW JONES INDUSTRIAL SEASONAL INFLUENCES BY MONTH
content:
Let's break down the Dow Jones Industrial seasonal influences per calendar month:
- January: Typically a bearish month.
- February: Typically a bullish month.
- March: Generally seen as a consolidation month.
- April: Typically a bullish month.
- May: Typically a bearish month.
- June: A consolidation month, ending with a bearish tone.
- July: Bullish into the mid-year high.
- August: Generally seen as a consolidation month.
- September: Split between the first half being bullish and the second half being bearish.
- October: Usually makes the final quarter of the year's low. It can happen in September during the second
half. While bearish, it may drop to make a seasonal low there or in October, then trade aggressively higher.
- November: Typically a bullish month.
- December: Generally a Santa Claus rally bullish month.

Here we have the entire calendar year in broad, generic terms, indicating whether to expect higher or lower
prices. This information is derived from 20-year, 15-year, and 5-year averages.
If you observe the overall consolidations, expansions, trending, and non-trending periods, and when prices
go higher or not, the blue and red lines (representing different averages) are very closely correlated. In my
opinion, this bolsters confidence in the number crunching of seasonal tendencies. If it averages higher in
February over the last 20 years, and also over 15 years, it shows consistency across different timeframes
of data analysis. Consistency, however, is not high probability, perfection, fantasy, or absolute no-risk. It
means probabilities are historically speaking, and nothing is guaranteed by looking at the past. But if we
assume there's a pattern, and we're using seasonal tendencies, this is one worth investigating.

Breaking down the calendar months gives us a strong consensus for short-term or swing trading stocks,
and also for day trading the S&P for astute traders. We also have months where opportunities with high
probabilities are far less likely:
- March
- June
- August
These months typically won't be fruitful for high probability conditions.

There will always be aberrations where the market doesn't fit the seasonal tendency, and that's acceptable.
Many times, months suggested as bullish or bearish will be the opposite. This is largely based on
underlying trends or market environments.
Because the seasonal tendency highlights the underlying inclination for stocks to be purchased, bought,
and held, it will show the strongest buy-side seasonal tendencies. Thus, in a bullish market, the bullish
months will indicate the best opportunities for swing trading long stocks.

section_title: LEVERAGING BEARISH SEASONAL MONTHS FOR SHORT SELLING
content:
For bearish months, even during strong periods like the last 20 years when the stock market has been
generally going higher, if we identify typically bearish months like May, the second half of September, and
January, these can spell aggressive selling.
In bear markets, focusing on these bearish seasonal months when the market is generally moving lower or
the overall tide is falling could lead to supercharged short-selling opportunities. We can look for sellers in
weak stocks or be bearish on S&P trading.

We'll now look at a couple of case studies. I won't cover the entire calendar year; I encourage you to go to
barchart.com, pull up individual months yourself, and examine all that data. You can do this by inputting the
beginning and ending dates for each calendar month and using respective delivery contracts (March, June,
September, and December contracts). Refer to previous lessons for contract codes, how to pull up years,
and symbols.

FEBRUARY
- BULLISH SEASONAL TENDENCY
The first example is for February, which we know from the previous slide is generally a bullish month
seasonally. On the chart, we see the major stock averages: NASDAQ (top), E-mini S&P (middle), and Dow
Jones (bottom). I'm using futures contracts for representation, but cash prices would show the same thing.
Observe February 2nd and 3rd:
- NASDAQ made an equal low.
- S&P and Dow failed to make that equal low, instead forming higher lows.
This is our criteria for strong tendencies an unwillingness to go lower, which is our Index SMT (Smart
Money Tool) divergence, as discussed in S&P trading content. We see indices showing signs of smart
money accumulation.
Later in the month, around the 6th to 8th trading day:
- NASDAQ made a higher low.
- S&P made a lower low.
- Dow Jones made a slightly higher low.
Following this, we saw another movement higher across the averages.

MARCH
- CONSOLIDATION AND WHIPSAWS
Next, we look at March. In the shaded area, March is generally a consolidation period. It exhibits whipsaws
of higher and lower prices. More aggressively:
- The second week of March into the third week is generally bearish.
- It then starts to rally towards the close of March.
This is communicated by index divergences:
- NASDAQ making higher highs.
- S&P (middle) making lower highs.
- Dow Jones Futures making lower highs.
This led to a resulting sell-off. At the lows, between the 21st and 26th, you can observe a divergence
(which I encourage you to study yourself). NASDAQ showed a higher low compared to the lows seen in the
E-mini S&P and Dow futures contract. This was followed by a subsequent rally higher across the major
three averages. So, while it's a consolidation month, it doesn't mean there's no opportunity; it means you
need to understand the context generally, the entire month is consolidation.

APRIL
- BULLISH TENDENCY AND DIVERGENCE
Our next example is April. I have the contracts for NASDAQ (top), E-mini S&P (bottom), and Dow (center)
this time. Observe the divergence indicating smart money accumulation:
- NASDAQ failed to make a lower low.
- Dow went lower.
- S&P failed to go lower.
This index divergence preceded a nice movement higher. Simultaneously, mid-April aligns with the
seasonal tendency, starting with a slightly bearish tone and then vaulting aggressively up into the end of
April. This pattern is clearly seen across all averages.

MAY
- BEARISH SEASONAL TENDENCY
Our final example is May, which is generally a bearish month seasonally. Looking at the averages on the
right:
- E-mini S&P (top chart) makes a slightly higher high.
- Dow Futures fails to make a higher high.
- NASDAQ makes a higher high.
This leads to a sell-off into the midpoint, almost the third week of May. We then see a small flurry higher in
the seasonal tendency towards the close of the month. This creates an intra-month seasonal low. Overall,
however, May is generally a bearish month.

As a reminder, May 2017 is part of a larger consolidation seen in the year of this recording. It's been an
unorthodox stock market, finding higher highs, but doing so with formerly leading stocks losing their
momentum and not making new highs. The market is making higher highs, but without a lot of its previous
leadership.
There will be times when the stock market defies all logic, doing the opposite of what you expect, and vice
versa. If you're going to trade stocks, in my opinion, it's better to focus on times when the market is
predisposed to go higher, and not during periods like the 'bubble' I believe we are in during 2017.

section_title: THE POWER OF SELF-DIRECTED STOCK TRADING
content:
If you are a trader using investment vehicles like IRAs or retirement accounts, and it's possible for you
globally to manage it as a self-directed medium trading your own choices and selections of which stocks
to be in and when to exit I believe this will supercharge your returns. No one will do a better job than you
in caring for your money.
Most of us work hard for our money or obtain it through inheritance, so we care about not losing it.
Professionals at firms, supposedly looking out for our best interests, often aren't, and surprisingly, are not
contractually obligated to do so when examined closely.

section_title: DISPELLING INVESTMENT MYTHS AND TIMING THE MARKET
content:
The market always propels new participants. There's always a new crowd willing to invest because the idea
is perpetual: invest for the future, for tax deductions, deferment, and retire rich. Yet, major stock market
crashes and corrections occur, and many with paper profits end up with far less than they expected.
As an investor in stocks, I still believe you need to be a trader in stocks. There are times to be in stocks and
times to be out. This teaching focuses on when it's ideal, based on past cyclical, seasonal, and statistical
information, for things to come to fruition. By focusing on these 'sweet spots' for investing, it will hopefully
be more advantageous than simply buying stocks because someone on TV advises it.

section_title: OUTPERFORMING THE MARKET WITH ICT CONCEPTS
content:
If we use our own analysis and achieve consistent returns that outpace and outperform the market, which I
believe the concepts taught this week will enable, we'll do better than general averages. There are many
inaccuracies regarding the stock market's average annual return due to complex number crunching. Forget
those ideas about expected returns; you'll likely perform differently, often outperforming your lowest
expectations, and sometimes even your highest.

As we go through this week's material, understand that it's aimed at:
- Providing another asset class if it interests you.
- Empowering self-directed management of retirement accounts, allowing you to pick and choose entry and
exit points for stocks.
Additional lessons on looking at stocks will be covered in an extra video after the fifth lesson, making it six
videos this week. I am confident that by the end of this week, you will know significantly more about stocks
than the average person, and certainly more than many online 'experts'. Until our next lesson, I wish you
good luck and good trading.
